select_when: A product, service, or workflow will fail unless it matches real user goals, frictions, and workarounds rather than internal assumptions.
The team needs to reframe the problem through observation, empathy work, or journey mapping before locking design commitments.
Human adoption risk is higher than technical uncertainty, so the main gap is understanding lived experience rather than shipping mechanics.
Iterative prototyping and test cycles can cheaply expose whether the current concept fits how users actually think and act.
avoid_when: User-centered language is being used decoratively while the real decision has already been made.
The team is generalizing from abstract personas or internal intuition without direct observation, testing, or contradiction-seeking.

curation_notes.summary: Kept the model centered on empathy-led reframing, journey understanding, and iterative fit testing rather than generic design-process branding.
donor_drops: Did not preserve broader service-design sprawl because the raw source is strongest on perspective shift, prototype feedback, and anti-assumption discipline.
open_questions: The output label compresses a source that spans problem definition, prototype testing, and user-adoption logic.

select_when.note: The source is explicit on user-fit failure, empathy and journey work, adoption risk, and iterative prototyping as the strongest activation cues.

avoid_when.note: Both anti-activation conditions are directly grounded in the source warnings about decorative empathy and assumption-driven design.

input_type.note: The source consistently activates around human-centered design ambiguity, while the compact label is reviewed normalization.

output_type.note: The raw playbook yields reframed user-fit design direction; the output label is a compact reviewed abstraction.

reasoning_types.note: The model diagnoses unmet needs and reasons causally about how design choices change adoption, while the taxonomy labels remain normalized.
